result:
I. The Moment Before the Page

There is a hush that arrives just before the fingers touch the keys, a pocket of silence so complete it feels almost criminal to break it. It is the same hush that lingers in a theater when the houselights die but the curtain has not yet lifted, when every possibility is still alive and no story has yet been disqualified. I sit in that hush now, not because I know what I want to say, but because I have promised myself I will stay here until something honest crawls out of the dark. I have no outline, no thesis, no neat stack of corroborating citations—only the appetite to keep writing until the writing tells me why it began. If you have never written under those conditions, try it once: swear off every map, every lighthouse, every friendly voice that promises you a safe shore. You will discover that language behaves differently when it is not being steered. It eddies, it loops, it doubles back like a bloodhound that has lost the scent and must sniff out its own trail. You will also discover that you are not one person but a shifting parliament of selves, each with its own accent and agenda, and they will argue over every comma. The illusion of the sovereign “I” does not survive long in that parliament; it is voted out, reinstated, impeached again, all within the span of a paragraph.

II. The City Inside the Skull

Let me begin, then, with the city I carry under my hair. It is not the city where I pay rent, not the one whose buses I board with a predetermined ticket. It is the city that assembles itself when I close my eyes, a sprawling, contradictory metropolis whose street names change overnight and whose rivers sometimes run upward. The map is folded so small it fits between the hemispheres of my brain, yet when I unfold even a corner I can walk for hours. There is a café on the corner of Impermanence and Nostalgia where the waiters speak only in questions. “Would you like your memories reheated?” “Is that regret decaf or fully leaded?” The pastries are warm, but if you bite too eagerly you break a tooth on a decade you had tried to swallow whole. At dusk the lamps flicker in Morse code, spelling out the names of everyone who ever left the city, which is everyone, because no one is allowed to stay. The law is unwritten but absolute: residency is revoked the moment you believe you belong. And so we walk quickly, love quickly, erect monuments with foundations of wet sand, knowing the tide is already on its way. Yet the city is not cruel; it is simply allergic to stasis. Every morning the sun rises from a different architect’s blueprint, and every evening it sets into a demolished shrine. The only constant is the sound of construction, the jackhammer of the heart refusing to quit even when the building code is clearly posted: DO NOT ENTER, STRUCTURE UNSOUND.

III. A Brief History of Breath

Before language there was only the diaphragm, the obedient balloon inflating, deflating, keeping the newborn alive long enough to learn the first word. I try to remember this when I catch myself treating sentences like oxygen, as if to stop writing would be to suffocate. The lungs do not care about publication; they do not check the metrics of inhales. They serve, they release, they serve again, modest as monks. Somewhere in the Balkans there is a monastery where the monks have taken a vow of silence so absolute they do not even cleared their throats. Visitors assume the silence is empty, but it is actually crowded—crowded with every story the monks refused to spend aloud, every temptation to explain themselves. The abbott once told a traveler, “We do not keep silence; silence keeps us.” I think of that line whenever I notice how eagerly I rush to fill the quiet between two people, as if silence were a crack in the hull and words the only caulk. What if, instead, I let the quiet widen until it becomes a canyon we could both stand inside without touching? Would we hear the river at the bottom, the one that has been running since before breath, before speech, before any of us needed to prove we were here?

IV. The Museum of Unfinished Things

In the city inside my skull there is also a museum with no roof. It is open to the weather; rain smears the ink on exhibit cards, snow accumulates on half-carved statues. Every visitor is handed a flashlight at the entrance and instructed to “shine it on whatever terrifies you.” Most people aim the beam at the abortive canvases, the novels stopped mid-sentence, the marriages dissolved before the thank-you notes were mailed. But the truly unsettling displays are the ones that look finished: the violin that only plays one note, the clock whose hands move but whose numbers have been replaced by questions, the mirror that reflects your twelve-year-old face superimposed on your present one. In the gift shop you can buy a snow globe that, once shaken, will not settle; the plastic flakes remain in perpetual swirl, a blizzard you can carry in your palm. I own one. Some nights I shake it and place it on the nightstand, a private apocalypse to watch until I fall asleep. The trick is to accept that the snow will never land; the trick is to stop waiting.

V. On the Impossibility of Linear Time When You Are the Grandchild of Refugees

My grandmother’s suitcase is smaller than a child’s schoolbag. It is made of cardboard once, now reinforced with packing tape the color of dried blood. Inside, when she opens it for me one afternoon in her Toronto basement, are not clothes but layers of time: the tablecloth her mother embroidered the week the border guards changed the name of their village, the photograph of her brother who disappeared into the same forest where they used to gather mushrooms, the salt shaker shaped like a swan she grabbed from the dining room because it was the only thing not nailed down. She closes the lid quickly, as if the objects might leap out and demand asylum. “We were not meant to stay anywhere,” she says, shrugging. The shrug contains every map she burned in her mind so the enemy could not read them. Years later I will understand that memory itself is a refugee, always crossing, never arrived. It smuggles itself across the checkpoints of official history, bribing the guards with small sentimental coins. When I try to write her story chronologically, the pages rearrange themselves like passengers jockeying for a place on the last train out. Event A refuses to board before Event B; Event C disembarks early and hitches a ride on a different narrative altogether. I give up and let the suitcase speak in its own word order: swan, forest, shrug, border, salt.

VI. The Year of the Borrowed Heart

At twenty-three I loved someone whose heart had been replaced with a mechanical valve. She carried a card in her wallet that certified the device, printed with a warning to airport security: “Metal artifact will set off detectors.” We used to joke that she was part cyborg, but the truth was subtler: she had to listen for a sound that was not her own. The valve clicked once every second, a tiny metronome keeping 60 beats per minute. In bed at night I pressed my ear to her sternum and heard time itself ticking inside her, indifferent to love or ambition or the price of tomatoes. The intimacy of that sound unmakes you; you realize your own pulse is a feral drum by comparison, sloppy and devotional. When she left me for a woman who restored antique clocks, I could not blame her; she had simply traded up to a more sophisticated mechanism. For months afterward I confused every distant clock with the memory of her body. I removed the batteries from my bedside alarm. I walked through the world expecting to hear the click and being devastated when it did not come. One afternoon in a thrift store I found a wind-up pocket watch that ticked at exactly sixty beats. I bought it and carried it in my breast pocket, a transplanted ghost. The year of the borrowed heart ended the day I forgot to wind the watch. It stopped at 3:17 p.m. while I was on a subway platform. I felt the silence bloom across my chest like a bruise, and then—unexpectedly—relief. The remedy had finally become a souvenir.

VII. The Cartography of Smell

Scientists say olfaction is the sense most tightly braided to memory, but they rarely add that it is also the most traitorous. A single molecule can smuggle you across decades without warning. Last week the supermarket stacked a pyramid of apricots near the entrance, and suddenly I was seven, standing in my grandfather’s orchard, the ground littered with fallen suns. I could taste the metal of the bucket handle, feel the gauze of the mosquito net scratching my neck. The apricots in the store were hard, odorless, bred for transit. Still, the ghost scent slipped through: a crack in the wall of time wide enough for my entire body to tumble through. I stood there blocking the aisle, eyes wet, while strangers maneuvered their carts around me like water parting for a boulder. No other sense performs this ambush so efficiently. A song can transport you, yes, but you choose to press play; the apricot attacks uninvited. I imagine a parallel atlas where countries are not bordered by rivers or mountain ridges but by smell. The Republic of Benzoin, the Confederacy of Wet Wool, the United Provinces of Iron-Rain-on-Pavement. Travel visas would be issued not by governments but by grandmothers, each stamping your passport with a different childhood dish. Crossing into the Territory of Cardamom would require you to remove your shoes and recount the first time you were betrayed. The map would be useless to the noseless, which is to say, to anyone who believes that history is only what can be photographed.

VIII. The Paradox of the Written Voice

Write like you speak, they advise, as if the mouth were a reliable stenographer. But my speaking voice cracks on long vowels, trips over sibilants, abandons sentences halfway when it realizes they are heading nowhere. On the page I can loop a clause like a magician’s scarf, pull yards of syntax out of a sleeve, make clauses pirouette in ways that would leave my tongue hospitalized. The written voice is both more naked and more costumed: it stands still long enough for you to notice its pores, yet it is also wearing every word it has ever read. Sometimes I meet readers who insist they can “hear” me in the text, and I wonder whose ear they are using. The one that once overheard me arguing with a landlord? The one that eavesdropped on the 3 a.m. conversation when I confessed to being afraid of dying? Or are they hearing a composite chord—my grandmother’s sigh pitched over my father’s lecture, my former lover’s laugh braided into the way I truncate gerunds? The written voice is a ventriloquist that throws its sound into the reader’s skull and then pretends the echo was there all along. I used to feel guilty about this ventriloquism, as if I were smuggling contraband intimacy. Now I think of it as hospitality: I build a room inside the sentence, leave the key under the mat, and hope you will move your own furniture in.

IX. The Archive of Dreams I Never Told Anyone

1. I am being chased through a library where the shelves are made of ice. Every book I touch freezes my fingertips, but I keep opening them because the pages contain instructions for how to stop being afraid.

2. My teeth fall out one by one, but instead of blood there is seawater. I collect the teeth in a jar and later plant them in a garden. Overnight they grow into tiny ivory windmills that generate just enough electricity to power a night-light.

3. I discover a hidden door in my childhood bedroom that leads to the exact same room, only thirty seconds ahead in time. I use the lag to correct embarrassing remarks, but eventually the thirty seconds collapse and I am stuck stuttering mid-sentence forever.

Interpreters reduce these to anxiety, to loss of control, to fear of aging. But interpreters are tourists; they leave before the city changes its laws. What if the ice library is not a metaphor but a climate? What if the seawater is not emotion but literal ocean, and my mouth a tidepool? I keep these dreams in a drawer the way a sailor keeps unopened letters from home—proof that somewhere there is a shore that still recognizes him, even if he can no longer read the language.

X. The Ethics of Inhabiting Another’s Pain

Write what you know, they also say, a commandment that would leave most of us recounting commute routes and gastrointestinal grumbles. The trick is to recognize how little you know, and then to treat that ignorance as a plot of land you are forbidden to build on but allowed to garden. You can plant questions, water them with attention, and wait to see what volunteer seedlings appear. When they do, you must ask permission before you harvest them into metaphor. Some pains are not yours to aestheticize: the rattle of a child’s empty stomach in a warzone, the rasp of a knee on asphalt under a cop’s weight. You can witness, you can amplify, you can redirect your tax dollars and your vote, but you cannot costume those pains as your own without becoming a thief. The line between solidarity and colonization is thin and illuminated only in retrospect. I have crossed it more than once, swaggering in with my adjectives and my tidy epiphanies. The apology I owe is larger than this essay, larger than any essay. It is a silence I must keep learning to speak.

XI. The Solace of Obsolete Technologies

I own a typewriter manufactured in 1953, the year DNA’s double helix was discovered and my parents were still unborn. The typewriter weighs slightly less than a Thanksgiving turkey and smells like the inside of a piano. Each keystroke is a small detonation; the letters slam into the paper with the violence of arrival. You cannot lie on a typewriter; the clatter is too honest. A delete key does not exist; you must confront your error, decide whether to X it out, to retype the entire page, to let the mistake stand like a cowbird in the nest. The machine forces a slower morality. I use it when the sentences inside me feel too slick, when the word-processor’s cursor blinks like a impatient parent. I feed the typewriter a virgin sheet and feel the hunger of its teeth. By the time I reach the bottom margin I am usually calmer, as if the hammers have beaten my anxiety into the paper’s fibers. The resulting text is never perfect, but it is irrevocably there, a small monument to the moment I stopped flinching.

XII. The Children We Refuse to Have

My friends are dividing into two tribes: those who reproduce and those who apologize for not reproducing. Both speak of the future in the language of forecast, as if it were a storm they must either shelter or abandon. I dream of a third tribe: those who raise the children that already exist in the world’s neglected corners, who tutor, who foster, who send monthly sums to organizations whose names are asterisks in the news. But even this dream risks self-congratulation, the NGO savior complex that snaps photos of itself handing out bottled water. Perhaps the truest refusal is to nurture what cannot be named yet: the ideas that will need midwives in a century, the songs that will germinate only after every current language is dead. I imagine a classroom on the moon where the lesson plan is blank and the chalk is made of compressed stardust. The students are not human; they are the inheritors of whatever consciousness evolves after the oceans finish acidifying. I teach them one word: remember. I pronounce it in every accent I can still recall from my grandmother’s kitchen. They repeat it back in frequencies too low for my terrestrial ear, but I feel the vibration in my bones, a promise that something of us will linger, even if it is only the tremor of a syllable.

XIII. The Liturgy of Small Repetitions

Every morning I grind coffee beans while the kettle climbs toward its single note of panic. The grinder’s burr rotates exactly 473 times; I have counted. The aroma rises, and with it the day’s first coherent thought: I am still here. The ritual is otherwise meaningless—no deity tallies my adherence, no cosmic ledger notes the exact coarseness of the grind. Yet I perform it like a priest who has forgotten the doctrine but remembers the choreography. Across the hall my neighbor performs her own liturgy: three clicks of the deadbolt, two shakes of the yoga mat, one throat-clearing that sounds like a zipper half-closing. We are both authoring stability in the dialect of repetition. Psychologists call this “conditioned resilience,” but the Latin fails to capture the tenderness, the way the body learns to parent itself. When the world’s horrors scroll across my screen—another shooting, another glacier calving into the sea—I close the laptop and return to the kettle, the grinder, the count. The beans spill into the hopper like brown planets settling into orbit. For thirty seconds the cosmos makes sense, and sometimes thirty seconds is enough.

XIV. The Promise of the Unsent Letter

There is a folder on my desktop labeled “Burn Before Reading” that contains 187 letters I will never send. They are addressed to ex-lovers, to the teacher who said I would never amount to anything, to the version of myself who stood on a bridge at 2 a.m. and considered the drop. Writing them is not catharsis; it is archaeology. I excavate the strata of who I was when the wounds were still open, when the grammar shook from adrenaline. Some letters are all profanity, others all apology, a few are single sentences: “I can finally listen to that song without bleeding.” The folder is encrypted, but the password is the same as the Wi-Fi network of the first apartment I ever lived alone in—a petty charm against anyone who might trespass. Once a year I scroll through the files, updating the diction, deleting metaphors that now feel ornate. The exercise is like repainting a house no one will ever enter. I do not delete the folder; I do not print it out. The unsent letter is a candle that consumes itself yet keeps burning, a miracle of physics and denial. It proves I can contain the explosion, that language can be both fuse and firebreak.

XV. The Horizon as Shared Hallucination

Stand on any shore and you will witness the same hoax: the earth pretending to end. Sailors know the ruse, yet they still speak of “going over” as if the planet were a flat table and the horizon its literal edge. We need the lie; without it the eye would drown in information. I think of this when people claim we live in a post-truth era, as if truth were ever anything more than a consensus hallucination sturdy enough to build grocery stores upon. The horizon is where we practice our first surrender: here the eye stops, the mind continues. Every religion, every scientific theorem, every poem is an attempt to guess what happens after the eye gives up. Some say angels, some say dark energy, some say the poem itself. The only heresy is to insist the answer is final. The horizon recedes at the exact speed we approach it, a magician’s assistant forever ahead of the reveal. I find this perversely comforting: that we will never run out of distance, that the edge will keep escaping us like the last word of a sentence that refuses to be caught.

XVI. The Quiet After the Last Page

Eventually the fingers slow, the kettle cools, the city inside the skull dims its streetlights one by one. The parliament of selves votes to adjourn; the dissenting minority puts out its cigarette and shuffles home. What remains is not silence but a lower frequency of conversation, the kind dogs and philosophers can hear. I read back over the 2,500 words and notice the places where I lied, where I prettified the grief, where I let the metaphors grow like ivy over a crack in the foundation. I leave the lies intact; they are part of the architecture now, load-bearing delusions. Outside, the real city’s garbage trucks begin their predawn route, metal jaws grinding the evidence of yesterday’s hunger. I think of the typewriter downstairs, its ribbon soaked with the oil of vanished alphabets. I think of the unsent letters, the mechanical heartbeat I no longer carry, the apricots that will rot before they ever ripen into memory. I think of you, stranger, who have followed me this far without a map, and who will now close the tab, or shut the book, or simply let the screen go dark. Between us the horizon continues its retreat. May it keep retreating. May we never catch it. May we never stop trying.

A delete key does not exist; you must confront your error, decide whether to X it out, to retype the entire page, to let the mistake stand like a cowbird in the nest. The machine forces a slower morality. I use it when the sentences inside me feel too slick, when the word-processor’s cursor blinks like a impatient parent. I feed the typewriter a virgin sheet and feel the hunger of its teeth. By the time I reach the bottom margin I am usually calmer, as if the hammers have beaten my anxiety into the paper’s fibers. The resulting text is never perfect, but it is irrevocably there, a small monument to the moment I stopped flinching.

XII. The Children We Refuse to Have

My friends are dividing into two tribes: those who reproduce and those who apologize for not reproducing. Both speak of the future in the language of forecast, as if it were a storm they must either shelter or abandon. I dream of a third tribe: those who raise the children that already exist in the world’s neglected corners, who tutor, who foster, who send monthly sums to organizations whose names are asterisks in the news. But even this dream risks self-congratulation, the NGO savior complex that snaps photos of itself handing out bottled water. Perhaps the truest refusal is to nurture what cannot be named yet: the ideas that will need midwives in a century, the songs that will germinate only after every current language is dead. I imagine a classroom on the moon where the lesson plan is blank and the chalk is made of compressed stardust. The students are not human; they are the inheritors of whatever consciousness evolves after the oceans finish acidifying. I teach them one word: remember. I pronounce it in every accent I can still recall from my grandmother’s kitchen. They repeat it back in frequencies too low for my terrestrial ear, but I feel the vibration in my bones, a promise that something of us will linger, even if it is only the tremor of a syllable.

XIII. The Liturgy of Small Repetitions

Every morning I grind coffee beans while the kettle climbs toward its single note of panic. The grinder’s burr rotates exactly 473 times; I have counted. The aroma rises, and with it the day’s first coherent thought: I am still here. The ritual is otherwise meaningless—no deity tallies my adherence, no cosmic ledger notes the exact coarseness of the grind. Yet I perform it like a priest who has forgotten the doctrine but remembers the choreography. Across the hall my neighbor performs her own liturgy: three clicks of the deadbolt, two shakes of the yoga mat, one throat-clearing that sounds like a zipper half-closing. We are both authoring stability in the dialect of repetition. Psychologists call this “conditioned resilience,” but the Latin fails to capture the tenderness, the way the body learns to parent itself. When the world’s horrors scroll across my screen—another shooting, another glacier calving into the sea—I close the laptop and return to the kettle, the grinder, the count. The beans spill into the hopper like brown planets settling into orbit. For thirty seconds the cosmos makes sense, and sometimes thirty seconds is enough.

XIV. The Promise of the Unsent Letter

There is a folder on my desktop labeled “Burn Before Reading” that contains 187 letters I will never send. They are addressed to ex-lovers, to the teacher who said I would never amount to anything, to the version of myself who stood on a bridge at 2 a.m. and considered the drop. Writing them is not catharsis; it is archaeology. I excavate the strata of who I was when the wounds were still open, when the grammar shook from adrenaline. Some letters are all profanity, others all apology, a few are single sentences: “I can finally listen to that song without bleeding.” The folder is encrypted, but the password is the same as the Wi-Fi network of the first apartment I ever lived alone in—a petty charm against anyone who might trespass. Once a year I scroll through the files, updating the diction, deleting metaphors that now feel ornate. The exercise is like repainting a house no one will ever enter. I do not delete the folder; I do not print it out. The unsent letter is a candle that consumes itself yet keeps burning, a miracle of physics and denial. It proves I can contain the explosion, that language can be both fuse and firebreak.

XV. The Horizon as Shared Hallucination

Stand on any shore and you will witness the same hoax: the earth pretending to end. Sailors know the ruse, yet they still speak of “going over” as if the planet were a flat table and the horizon its literal edge. We need the lie; without it the eye would drown in information. I think of this when people claim we live in a post-truth era, as if truth were ever anything more than a consensus hallucination sturdy enough to build grocery stores upon. The horizon is where we practice our first surrender: here the eye stops, the mind continues. Every religion, every scientific theorem, every poem is an attempt to guess what happens after the eye gives up. Some say angels, some say dark energy, some say the poem itself. The only heresy is to insist the answer is final. The horizon recedes at the exact speed we approach it, a magician’s assistant forever ahead of the reveal. I find this perversely comforting: that we will never run out of distance, that the edge will keep escaping us like the last word of a sentence that refuses to be caught.

XVI. The Quiet After the Last Page

Eventually the fingers slow, the kettle cools, the city inside the skull dims its streetlights one by one. The parliament of selves votes to adjourn; the dissenting minority puts out its cigarette and shuffles home. What remains is not silence but a lower frequency of conversation, the kind dogs and philosophers can hear. I read back over the 2,500 words and notice the places where I lied, where I prettified the grief, where I let the metaphors grow like ivy over a crack in the foundation. I leave the lies intact; they are part of the architecture now, load-bearing delusions. Outside, the real city’s garbage trucks begin their predawn route, metal jaws grinding the evidence of yesterday’s hunger. I think of the typewriter downstairs, its ribbon soaked with the oil of vanished alphabets. I think of the unsent letters, the mechanical heartbeat I no longer carry, the apricots that will rot before they ever ripen into memory. I think of you, stranger, who have followed me this far without a map, and who will now close the tab, or shut the book, or simply let the screen go dark. Between us the horizon continues its retreat. May it keep retreating. May we never catch it. May we never stop trying.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
